{{ill|lt=Surugakuni|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Sannomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}
Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} |Alternative name = Miho Daimyojin |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=November 1st|Q2997|WD=Q2997|1=November 1}} |Shinto ritual = Tsutsugayu Festival ({{ill|lt=February 14th|Q2332|WD=Q2332|1=February 14}}-{{ill|lt=February 14th|Q2332|WD=Q2332|lt=15th|1=February 14}})|Map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|lat_deg=35|lat_min=0|lat_sec=0.40|lon_deg=138|lon_min=31|lon_sec=15.16|label=Miho Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|0|0.40|N|138|31|15.16|E}}}} }} [[File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] ``Miho Shrine'' is located in {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Shizuoka City|Q174691|WD=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}}{{ill|lt=Shimizu Ward|Q79385|WD=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}} Miho. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Sannomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}, {{ill|lt=modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}}.It is known as a shrine related to ``Hagoromo no Matsu'' in {{ill|lt=Miho no Matsubara|Q869275|WD=Q869275|lt=Miho no Matsubara|1=Miho no Matsubara}}. The character ``Miho'' is also used for ``Miho'', ``Miho'', and ``Miho'' {{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Magazine Materials|1912}}. The shrine precincts and the approach (the path of the gods) are {{ill|lt=Cultural Heritage Sites (World Heritage)|Q210272|WD=Q210272|lt=World Cultural Heritage|1=Cultural heritage}} [http://www.shizuoka-citypromotion.jp/mihonomatsubara/learn/area.html Constituent assets Miho no Matsubara Range] (Shizuoka City). . === Enshrined God === The following two deities are enshrined. . :* '''{{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ookunushi|1=Ōkuninushi}}''' (Oanamuchi Mikoto, {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}}) - Also known as ``Mihotsuhiko no Mikoto.'' :* '''{{ill|lt=Mihotsuhime|Q6378698|WD=Q6378698|lt=Mihotsuhime no Mikoto|1=Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto}}''' (Mihotsuhime no Mikoto) In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmeicho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmeicho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, there is one deity enshrined. Also, in "Suruga Zoshi", the two pillars are "Miho Tsuhiko" and "Miho Tsuhime" {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. === History === === Founding === [[File:Port of Shimizu and Miho Coast Areas Aerial photograph.jpg|thumb|220px|{{center|Aerial photograph around the Miho Peninsula}}{{Small|Composite of 12 photos taken in 1983. {{National Aerial Photography}}. The corner to the right of the center of the image is the precincts of Miho Shrine, and a row of pine trees extends from the precincts to {{ill|lt=Miho no Matsubara|Q869275|WD=Q869275|1=Miho no Matsubara}} on the coast. }}]] Founding date is unknown. In "Suruga Zoshi", it is said that {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} donated government money and shrine territory by imperial order to Mihosaki, Izumo Province (currently {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Matsue City|Q207321|WD=Q207321|1=Matsue}}It is also said that the deity was relocated from Mihonoseki Town), but it is not clear {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}.{{ill|lt=Miho no Matsubara|Q869275|WD=Q869275|lt=Miho no Matsubara|1=Miho no Matsubara}} has the ``Hagoromo no Matsu'', and from Hagoromo no Matsu to the head of Miho Shrine there is a line of pine trees, but this tree-lined road is It is said to be the path for the gods who descended from the pine trees to reach Miho Shrine, and is called the ``Kami no Michi'' {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. Even today, during the Tsutsugayu Shinto ritual, a ceremony is held on the shore to welcome the gods, and at that time, the Himorogi that the gods rely on are brought into the precincts through a row of pine trees.{{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. From these, the rituals at Miho Shrine are thought to be part of the ``Eternal Faith'' that welcomes the gods from the ``{{ill|lt=Eternal Country|Q11060259|WD=Q11060259|lt=Eternal Country|1=Tokoyo no kuni}}'' beyond the sea.{{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. === Outline === In the {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|lt=Kokukushi|1=Rikkokushi}}, the name of the god is ``Mikugami'', and {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}}7 years ({{ill|lt=865|Q30350|WD=Q30350|1=865}}) from the lower junior fifth rank to the upper junior fifth rank, later {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|WD=Q747451|1=Gangyō}}3 years ({{ja There is an article that states that {{ill|lt=Divine Grade|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} was promoted to the lower rank of Shogoi in 879|Q25001}}.{{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. {{ill|lt=extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}} It is described as ``Miho Shrine'' in Rubara County ({{ill|lt=Ibara District|Q988501|WD=Q988501|1=Ihara District, Shizuoka}}) and is listed as {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}.In ``{{ill|lt=Suruga Province Fudoki|Q11668491|WD=Q11668491|1=Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}'', it is written that ``Japanese Bushihohoku Kansei, Shikken Keida Gohyakuune, Tamekuni no Sangu Shrine.'' Therefore, our company is {{jalink|Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha Shrine|Q653180} in Suruga Province. } ({{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}}), it is presumed to have held the position of the third shrine after {{ill|lt=Toyozumi Shrine|Q18337536|WD=Q18337536|1=Toyozumi Shrine}} (Ninomiya) {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=151-152}}. However, the basis for writing this in the same article is not clear {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=151-152}}.Among the historical materials of the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}}, there is a crocodile mouth in the collection of Iimurono Shrine, dated 3 years ({{ill|lt=1319|Q5767|WD=Q5767|1=1319}}) {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. At this crocodile entrance, it looks like ``Miho Daimyojin,'' and it is said that it was dedicated by the priest's son and wife {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. In addition, in {{ill|lt=Oan|Q1146245|WD=Q1146245|1=Ōan}}3 ({{ill|lt=1370|Q6217|WD=Q6217|1=1370}}), Date Keimune was ordered to prohibit the felling of sacred trees and search for violators, and {{ill|lt=Koji (Japan)|Q1143910|WD=Q1143910|lt=Koji|1=Kōji (Muromachi period)}} In the 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1557|Q6595|WD=Q6595|1=1557}}), {{ill|lt=Yamashina Yotsugu|Q11470822|WD=Q11470822|1=Yamashina Tokitsugu}} was {{ill|lt=now At the recommendation of Kawa Yoshimoto|Q1054305|WD=Q1054305|1=Imagawa Yoshimoto}}, he visited a shrine marked as ``Miura no Daimyojin'' and paid his respects to the Ota clan (Ota clan) of Negi.{{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. In {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 5th year ({{ill|lt=1577|Q6704|WD=Q6704|1=1577}}), Kisaburo Ota was appointed as the chief priest of Miho, and the Daimyojin offering of the shrine, Matsubaraura et al., was relieved.{{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}.In the {{ill|lt=Early modern period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}, 106 stones from the three villages of Miho, Orito, and Betsufu were given as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shuinchi|1=UNKNOWN}} by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, and the shrine building was was also maintained, but it is said to have been destroyed by fire in {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}}8 ({{ill|lt=1668|Q7595|WD=Q7595|1=1668}}) {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} It was ranked as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, and in 1898|Q7848}}, it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}.{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Shiryo|1912}}. === God level === * Record of {{ill|lt=Shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} dedication in the {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|1=Rikkokushi}} era {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}} ** December 21, 7th year of {{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} ({{ill|lt=865|Q30350|WD=Q30350|1=865}}), from the lower junior fifth rank to the upper fifth rank (``{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'') - The spelling is ``Mirojin.'' ** April 5th, {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|WD=Q747451|1=Gangyō}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=879|Q25001|WD=Q25001|1=879}}), from the top of the Jugoi to the lower of the Shogoi ("Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku") - Written as "Mikujin". * After Six Kingdoms History ** Junior Second Rank (``Suruga Province Jinmyocho'') {{Sfn|Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries|2000|p=151-152}} - Written as "Miho Myojin." === Precincts === [[File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) honden.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main shrine (Shizuoka City designated cultural property)}}]] The shrine was built by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, but it was destroyed by fire in {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}}8 ({{ill|lt=1668|Q7595|WD=Q7595|1=1668}}) It is said to have been {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}, and was later built as a temporary shrine (karimiya) in the middle of the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} and continues to this day. The current main shrine is {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} and is designated as a cultural property by Shizuoka City. From the head of the shrine, a row of pine trees that are about 300 to 400 years old stretches for about 520 meters, called the "path of the gods," until the end of the row of pine trees {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Miho no Matsubara|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Noh song|Q8062662|WD=Q8062662|1=Yōkyoku}} ``Hagoromo no Matsu'', known for ``Hagoromo'', stands {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. The pine trees in Hagoromo have been replaced repeatedly due to withering and death, but it is presumed that the original pine trees were an extension of the tree-lined path {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) shaden.JPG|Hotel File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) sandou.JPG|Pine trees along the approach (God's path)File:Miho-no-Matsubara Hagoromo-no-matsu.JPG|Hagoromo no Matsu
{{Small|It is said that a god descends from the land of eternal life.{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yorishiro|1=UNKNOWN}}. }} === Setsusha === [[File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) Koyasu-jinja.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Koyasu Shrine}}]] * Koyasu Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Susanoo no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inada Hime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} * 5 companies in Maiden East ** Gofukuno Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Chojiraha God|Q11653519|WD=Q11653519|lt=Choshiraha Mikoto|1=Nagashirahano no Kami}} ** Inari Shrine - Deities enshrined: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Uka no Mitama-kami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Omiyahime-kami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Ota Mikoto|Q97496337|WD=Q97496337|1=Ōta-no-Mikoto}} ** Kofudayu Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Koshiro Nushi no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} ** Isomae Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukuhikomakami|1=UNKNOWN}} ** Utsurei Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Koou Utreimiko|UNKNOWN|lt=Kou Utreimyo}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kou Utreimyo|1=UNKNOWN}} * 3 companies west of the main hall ** Hachiman Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} ** Yakumo Shrine - Enshrined deity: Susano no Mikoto ** Shinmeisha - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}} * Haguruma Shrine - Enshrined in the pine tree of Hagoromo. File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) massha-1.JPG|5 subsidiary companies File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) massha-2.JPG|3 subsidiary companies File:Haguruma shrine.jpg|Haguruma shrine === Festival === ; {{ill|lt=regular festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} : {{ill|lt=November 1st|Q2997|WD=Q2997|1=November 1}} The most important festival of the year. During the festival, in addition to the ritual of hot water, a shrine maiden dance called ``Hagoromo no Mai'' is dedicated at the Maiden. This hagoromo dance is also dedicated at the autumn festival. ; Tsutsugayu festival : "Tsukayusai". A festival to pray for a good harvest that is held from the night of {{ill|lt=February 14th|Q2332|WD=Q2332|1=February 14}} to {{ill|lt=February 15th|Q2334|WD=Q2334|lt=15th|1=February 15}}. As part of the ritual, first a large cauldron is set up in front of the shrine and preparations are made to boil porridge. Then, in the middle of the night, a shrine is set up on the beach to welcome the gods, and a ceremony is performed by singing prayers without using any bonfires. The welcomed god resides in a Himorogi, and the Shinto priest carries the Himorogi through a row of pine trees to reach the shrine grounds. Afterwards, porridge is boiled in a large pot in front of the shrine, a bamboo tube is placed in it, and the amount of porridge in the tube is used to predict the year's crop. {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}} === Cultural assets === === Important cultural property (designated by the country) === * Tachi Mumei (attached to Itomaki Tachi Koshirae) (craft) *: Written by {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}. The blade is 95.2 cm long and the curve is 4.2 cm . Designated on April 30, 1921 (Taisho 10) {{Nationally Designated Cultural Properties Database|201|6198|Tachi Mumei}}. === Cultural property designated by Shizuoka City === *Tangible cultural property ** Main shrine (2 ridge tags, 1 pair of guardian dogs) (building) - Designated on February 23, 1996 {{PDFlink|[http://www.city.shizuoka.jp/000719631.pdf List of city-designated cultural properties]}} (Shizuoka City homepage). . ** 2 bamboo blinds (craft) - Designated on November 21, 1988 . === Others === * Rip of Feathers *: A slit that is said to be a piece of hagoromo that appears in the legend of hagoromo . It is believed to be a piece of Hire/Momo from over 1,200 years ago, and it is also assumed that it originated from the continent based on the pattern of the textile. * Hagoromo flute - A flute played during the Hagoromo dance. * Miho old picture map * Sanhoshaki === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Shizuoka City|Q174691|WD=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}}{{ill|lt=Shimizu Ward|Q79385|WD=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}}Miho 1073 '''Access''' * {{ill|lt=Tokai Passenger Railway|Q513679|WD=Q513679|1=Central Japan Railway Company}} (JR Tokai) {{ill|lt=Tokaido Main Line|Q1190152|WD=Q1190152|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}} {{ill|lt=Shimizu Station (Shizuoka Prefecture)|Q863065|WD=Q863065|lt=Shimizu Station|1=Shimizu Station (Shizuoka)}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Railway|Q5362040|WD=Q5362040|1=Shizuoka Railway}}{{ill|lt=Shizuoka Railway Shizuoka Shimizu Line|Q864249|WD=Q864249|lt=Shizuoka Shimizu Line|1=Shizuoka Railway Shizuoka–Shimizu Line}} {{ill|lt=Shin-Shimizu Station|Q7497132|WD=Q7497132|1=Shin-Shimizu Station}} ** Bus: Take {{ill|lt=Shizutetsu Just Line|Q11268847|WD=Q11268847|1=Shizutetsu Justline}} (bound for Miho) and get off at "Miho Matsubara Iriguchi" bus stop (about 25 minutes on board, about 5 minutes' walk after getting off) === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine history book * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|author=|year=1912|chapter=Miho Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Prefecture Gosha Meiji Shrine Materials|Q11486103|WD=Q11486103|1=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Materials|1912}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088244/884}} "Meiji Shrine Journal Materials Fuken Gosha Part 1"] (National Diet Library Digital Collection) Reference frames 884-885. * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor={{ill|lt=Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}}edited|author=Kanichi Nomoto|year=1987|chapter=Miho Shrine|title=Japanese Gods -Shrines and Sacred Sites- 10 Tokai|publisher={{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}|isbn=4560022208|ref={{Harvid|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} |Alternative name = Miho Daimyojin |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=November 1st|Q2997|WD=Q2997|1=November 1}} |Shinto ritual = Tsutsugayu Festival ({{ill|lt=February 14th|Q2332|WD=Q2332|1=February 14}}-{{ill|lt=February 14th|Q2332|WD=Q2332|lt=15th|1=February 14}})|Map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|lat_deg=35|lat_min=0|lat_sec=0.40|lon_deg=138|lon_min=31|lon_sec=15.16|label=Miho Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|0|0.40|N|138|31|15.16|E}}}} }} [[File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] ``Miho Shrine'' is located in {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Shizuoka City|Q174691|WD=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}}{{ill|lt=Shimizu Ward|Q79385|WD=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}} Miho. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Sannomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}, {{ill|lt=modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}}.It is known as a shrine related to ``Hagoromo no Matsu'' in {{ill|lt=Miho no Matsubara|Q869275|WD=Q869275|lt=Miho no Matsubara|1=Miho no Matsubara}}. The character ``Miho'' is also used for ``Miho'', ``Miho'', and ``Miho'' {{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Magazine Materials|1912}}. The shrine precincts and the approach (the path of the gods) are {{ill|lt=Cultural Heritage Sites (World Heritage)|Q210272|WD=Q210272|lt=World Cultural Heritage|1=Cultural heritage}} [http://www.shizuoka-citypromotion.jp/mihonomatsubara/learn/area.html Constituent assets Miho no Matsubara Range] (Shizuoka City). . === Enshrined God === The following two deities are enshrined. . :* '''{{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ookunushi|1=Ōkuninushi}}''' (Oanamuchi Mikoto, {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}}) - Also known as ``Mihotsuhiko no Mikoto.'' :* '''{{ill|lt=Mihotsuhime|Q6378698|WD=Q6378698|lt=Mihotsuhime no Mikoto|1=Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto}}''' (Mihotsuhime no Mikoto) In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmeicho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmeicho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, there is one deity enshrined. Also, in "Suruga Zoshi", the two pillars are "Miho Tsuhiko" and "Miho Tsuhime" {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. === History === === Founding === [[File:Port of Shimizu and Miho Coast Areas Aerial photograph.jpg|thumb|220px|{{center|Aerial photograph around the Miho Peninsula}}{{Small|Composite of 12 photos taken in 1983. {{National Aerial Photography}}. The corner to the right of the center of the image is the precincts of Miho Shrine, and a row of pine trees extends from the precincts to {{ill|lt=Miho no Matsubara|Q869275|WD=Q869275|1=Miho no Matsubara}} on the coast. }}]] Founding date is unknown. In "Suruga Zoshi", it is said that {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} donated government money and shrine territory by imperial order to Mihosaki, Izumo Province (currently {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Matsue City|Q207321|WD=Q207321|1=Matsue}}It is also said that the deity was relocated from Mihonoseki Town), but it is not clear {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}.{{ill|lt=Miho no Matsubara|Q869275|WD=Q869275|lt=Miho no Matsubara|1=Miho no Matsubara}} has the ``Hagoromo no Matsu'', and from Hagoromo no Matsu to the head of Miho Shrine there is a line of pine trees, but this tree-lined road is It is said to be the path for the gods who descended from the pine trees to reach Miho Shrine, and is called the ``Kami no Michi'' {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. Even today, during the Tsutsugayu Shinto ritual, a ceremony is held on the shore to welcome the gods, and at that time, the Himorogi that the gods rely on are brought into the precincts through a row of pine trees.{{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. From these, the rituals at Miho Shrine are thought to be part of the ``Eternal Faith'' that welcomes the gods from the ``{{ill|lt=Eternal Country|Q11060259|WD=Q11060259|lt=Eternal Country|1=Tokoyo no kuni}}'' beyond the sea.{{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. === Outline === In the {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|lt=Kokukushi|1=Rikkokushi}}, the name of the god is ``Mikugami'', and {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}}7 years ({{ill|lt=865|Q30350|WD=Q30350|1=865}}) from the lower junior fifth rank to the upper junior fifth rank, later {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|WD=Q747451|1=Gangyō}}3 years ({{ja There is an article that states that {{ill|lt=Divine Grade|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} was promoted to the lower rank of Shogoi in 879|Q25001}}.{{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. {{ill|lt=extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}} It is described as ``Miho Shrine'' in Rubara County ({{ill|lt=Ibara District|Q988501|WD=Q988501|1=Ihara District, Shizuoka}}) and is listed as {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}.In ``{{ill|lt=Suruga Province Fudoki|Q11668491|WD=Q11668491|1=Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}'', it is written that ``Japanese Bushihohoku Kansei, Shikken Keida Gohyakuune, Tamekuni no Sangu Shrine.'' Therefore, our company is {{jalink|Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha Shrine|Q653180} in Suruga Province. } ({{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}}), it is presumed to have held the position of the third shrine after {{ill|lt=Toyozumi Shrine|Q18337536|WD=Q18337536|1=Toyozumi Shrine}} (Ninomiya) {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=151-152}}. However, the basis for writing this in the same article is not clear {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=151-152}}.Among the historical materials of the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}}, there is a crocodile mouth in the collection of Iimurono Shrine, dated 3 years ({{ill|lt=1319|Q5767|WD=Q5767|1=1319}}) {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. At this crocodile entrance, it looks like ``Miho Daimyojin,'' and it is said that it was dedicated by the priest's son and wife {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. In addition, in {{ill|lt=Oan|Q1146245|WD=Q1146245|1=Ōan}}3 ({{ill|lt=1370|Q6217|WD=Q6217|1=1370}}), Date Keimune was ordered to prohibit the felling of sacred trees and search for violators, and {{ill|lt=Koji (Japan)|Q1143910|WD=Q1143910|lt=Koji|1=Kōji (Muromachi period)}} In the 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1557|Q6595|WD=Q6595|1=1557}}), {{ill|lt=Yamashina Yotsugu|Q11470822|WD=Q11470822|1=Yamashina Tokitsugu}} was {{ill|lt=now At the recommendation of Kawa Yoshimoto|Q1054305|WD=Q1054305|1=Imagawa Yoshimoto}}, he visited a shrine marked as ``Miura no Daimyojin'' and paid his respects to the Ota clan (Ota clan) of Negi.{{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. In {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 5th year ({{ill|lt=1577|Q6704|WD=Q6704|1=1577}}), Kisaburo Ota was appointed as the chief priest of Miho, and the Daimyojin offering of the shrine, Matsubaraura et al., was relieved.{{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}.In the {{ill|lt=Early modern period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}, 106 stones from the three villages of Miho, Orito, and Betsufu were given as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shuinchi|1=UNKNOWN}} by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, and the shrine building was was also maintained, but it is said to have been destroyed by fire in {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}}8 ({{ill|lt=1668|Q7595|WD=Q7595|1=1668}}) {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} It was ranked as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, and in 1898|Q7848}}, it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}.{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Shiryo|1912}}. === God level === * Record of {{ill|lt=Shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} dedication in the {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|1=Rikkokushi}} era {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}} ** December 21, 7th year of {{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} ({{ill|lt=865|Q30350|WD=Q30350|1=865}}), from the lower junior fifth rank to the upper fifth rank (``{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'') - The spelling is ``Mirojin.'' ** April 5th, {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|WD=Q747451|1=Gangyō}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=879|Q25001|WD=Q25001|1=879}}), from the top of the Jugoi to the lower of the Shogoi ("Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku") - Written as "Mikujin". * After Six Kingdoms History ** Junior Second Rank (``Suruga Province Jinmyocho'') {{Sfn|Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries|2000|p=151-152}} - Written as "Miho Myojin." === Precincts === [[File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) honden.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main shrine (Shizuoka City designated cultural property)}}]] The shrine was built by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, but it was destroyed by fire in {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}}8 ({{ill|lt=1668|Q7595|WD=Q7595|1=1668}}) It is said to have been {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}, and was later built as a temporary shrine (karimiya) in the middle of the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} and continues to this day. The current main shrine is {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} and is designated as a cultural property by Shizuoka City. From the head of the shrine, a row of pine trees that are about 300 to 400 years old stretches for about 520 meters, called the "path of the gods," until the end of the row of pine trees {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Miho no Matsubara|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Noh song|Q8062662|WD=Q8062662|1=Yōkyoku}} ``Hagoromo no Matsu'', known for ``Hagoromo'', stands {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. The pine trees in Hagoromo have been replaced repeatedly due to withering and death, but it is presumed that the original pine trees were an extension of the tree-lined path {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) shaden.JPG|Hotel File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) sandou.JPG|Pine trees along the approach (God's path)File:Miho-no-Matsubara Hagoromo-no-matsu.JPG|Hagoromo no Matsu
{{Small|It is said that a god descends from the land of eternal life.{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yorishiro|1=UNKNOWN}}. }} === Setsusha === [[File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) Koyasu-jinja.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Koyasu Shrine}}]] * Koyasu Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Susanoo no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inada Hime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} * 5 companies in Maiden East ** Gofukuno Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Chojiraha God|Q11653519|WD=Q11653519|lt=Choshiraha Mikoto|1=Nagashirahano no Kami}} ** Inari Shrine - Deities enshrined: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Uka no Mitama-kami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Omiyahime-kami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Ota Mikoto|Q97496337|WD=Q97496337|1=Ōta-no-Mikoto}} ** Kofudayu Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Koshiro Nushi no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} ** Isomae Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukuhikomakami|1=UNKNOWN}} ** Utsurei Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Koou Utreimiko|UNKNOWN|lt=Kou Utreimyo}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kou Utreimyo|1=UNKNOWN}} * 3 companies west of the main hall ** Hachiman Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} ** Yakumo Shrine - Enshrined deity: Susano no Mikoto ** Shinmeisha - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}} * Haguruma Shrine - Enshrined in the pine tree of Hagoromo. File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) massha-1.JPG|5 subsidiary companies File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) massha-2.JPG|3 subsidiary companies File:Haguruma shrine.jpg|Haguruma shrine === Festival === ; {{ill|lt=regular festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} : {{ill|lt=November 1st|Q2997|WD=Q2997|1=November 1}} The most important festival of the year. During the festival, in addition to the ritual of hot water, a shrine maiden dance called ``Hagoromo no Mai'' is dedicated at the Maiden. This hagoromo dance is also dedicated at the autumn festival. ; Tsutsugayu festival : "Tsukayusai". A festival to pray for a good harvest that is held from the night of {{ill|lt=February 14th|Q2332|WD=Q2332|1=February 14}} to {{ill|lt=February 15th|Q2334|WD=Q2334|lt=15th|1=February 15}}. As part of the ritual, first a large cauldron is set up in front of the shrine and preparations are made to boil porridge. Then, in the middle of the night, a shrine is set up on the beach to welcome the gods, and a ceremony is performed by singing prayers without using any bonfires. The welcomed god resides in a Himorogi, and the Shinto priest carries the Himorogi through a row of pine trees to reach the shrine grounds. Afterwards, porridge is boiled in a large pot in front of the shrine, a bamboo tube is placed in it, and the amount of porridge in the tube is used to predict the year's crop. {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}} === Cultural assets === === Important cultural property (designated by the country) === * Tachi Mumei (attached to Itomaki Tachi Koshirae) (craft) *: Written by {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}. The blade is 95.2 cm long and the curve is 4.2 cm . Designated on April 30, 1921 (Taisho 10) {{Nationally Designated Cultural Properties Database|201|6198|Tachi Mumei}}. === Cultural property designated by Shizuoka City === *Tangible cultural property ** Main shrine (2 ridge tags, 1 pair of guardian dogs) (building) - Designated on February 23, 1996 {{PDFlink|[http://www.city.shizuoka.jp/000719631.pdf List of city-designated cultural properties]}} (Shizuoka City homepage). . ** 2 bamboo blinds (craft) - Designated on November 21, 1988 . === Others === * Rip of Feathers *: A slit that is said to be a piece of hagoromo that appears in the legend of hagoromo . It is believed to be a piece of Hire/Momo from over 1,200 years ago, and it is also assumed that it originated from the continent based on the pattern of the textile. * Hagoromo flute - A flute played during the Hagoromo dance. * Miho old picture map * Sanhoshaki === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Shizuoka City|Q174691|WD=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}}{{ill|lt=Shimizu Ward|Q79385|WD=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}}Miho 1073 '''Access''' * {{ill|lt=Tokai Passenger Railway|Q513679|WD=Q513679|1=Central Japan Railway Company}} (JR Tokai) {{ill|lt=Tokaido Main Line|Q1190152|WD=Q1190152|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}} {{ill|lt=Shimizu Station (Shizuoka Prefecture)|Q863065|WD=Q863065|lt=Shimizu Station|1=Shimizu Station (Shizuoka)}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Railway|Q5362040|WD=Q5362040|1=Shizuoka Railway}}{{ill|lt=Shizuoka Railway Shizuoka Shimizu Line|Q864249|WD=Q864249|lt=Shizuoka Shimizu Line|1=Shizuoka Railway Shizuoka–Shimizu Line}} {{ill|lt=Shin-Shimizu Station|Q7497132|WD=Q7497132|1=Shin-Shimizu Station}} ** Bus: Take {{ill|lt=Shizutetsu Just Line|Q11268847|WD=Q11268847|1=Shizutetsu Justline}} (bound for Miho) and get off at "Miho Matsubara Iriguchi" bus stop (about 25 minutes on board, about 5 minutes' walk after getting off) === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine history book * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|author=|year=1912|chapter=Miho Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Prefecture Gosha Meiji Shrine Materials|Q11486103|WD=Q11486103|1=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Materials|1912}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088244/884}} "Meiji Shrine Journal Materials Fuken Gosha Part 1"] (National Diet Library Digital Collection) Reference frames 884-885. * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor={{ill|lt=Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}}edited|author=Kanichi Nomoto|year=1987|chapter=Miho Shrine|title=Japanese Gods -Shrines and Sacred Sites- 10 Tokai|publisher={{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}|isbn=4560022208|ref={{Harvid|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} |Alternative name = Miho Daimyojin |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=November 1st|Q2997|WD=Q2997|1=November 1}} |Shinto ritual = Tsutsugayu Festival ({{ill|lt=February 14th|Q2332|WD=Q2332|1=February 14}}-{{ill|lt=February 14th|Q2332|WD=Q2332|lt=15th|1=February 14}})|Map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|lat_deg=35|lat_min=0|lat_sec=0.40|lon_deg=138|lon_min=31|lon_sec=15.16|label=Miho Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|0|0.40|N|138|31|15.16|E}}}} }} [[File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] ``Miho Shrine'' is located in {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Shizuoka City|Q174691|WD=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}}{{ill|lt=Shimizu Ward|Q79385|WD=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}} Miho. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Sannomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}, {{ill|lt=modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}}.It is known as a shrine related to ``Hagoromo no Matsu'' in {{ill|lt=Miho no Matsubara|Q869275|WD=Q869275|lt=Miho no Matsubara|1=Miho no Matsubara}}. The character ``Miho'' is also used for ``Miho'', ``Miho'', and ``Miho'' {{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Magazine Materials|1912}}. The shrine precincts and the approach (the path of the gods) are {{ill|lt=Cultural Heritage Sites (World Heritage)|Q210272|WD=Q210272|lt=World Cultural Heritage|1=Cultural heritage}} [http://www.shizuoka-citypromotion.jp/mihonomatsubara/learn/area.html Constituent assets Miho no Matsubara Range] (Shizuoka City). . === Enshrined God === The following two deities are enshrined. . :* '''{{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ookunushi|1=Ōkuninushi}}''' (Oanamuchi Mikoto, {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}}) - Also known as ``Mihotsuhiko no Mikoto.'' :* '''{{ill|lt=Mihotsuhime|Q6378698|WD=Q6378698|lt=Mihotsuhime no Mikoto|1=Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto}}''' (Mihotsuhime no Mikoto) In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmeicho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmeicho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, there is one deity enshrined. Also, in "Suruga Zoshi", the two pillars are "Miho Tsuhiko" and "Miho Tsuhime" {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. === History === === Founding === [[File:Port of Shimizu and Miho Coast Areas Aerial photograph.jpg|thumb|220px|{{center|Aerial photograph around the Miho Peninsula}}{{Small|Composite of 12 photos taken in 1983. {{National Aerial Photography}}. The corner to the right of the center of the image is the precincts of Miho Shrine, and a row of pine trees extends from the precincts to {{ill|lt=Miho no Matsubara|Q869275|WD=Q869275|1=Miho no Matsubara}} on the coast. }}]] Founding date is unknown. In "Suruga Zoshi", it is said that {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} donated government money and shrine territory by imperial order to Mihosaki, Izumo Province (currently {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Matsue City|Q207321|WD=Q207321|1=Matsue}}It is also said that the deity was relocated from Mihonoseki Town), but it is not clear {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}.{{ill|lt=Miho no Matsubara|Q869275|WD=Q869275|lt=Miho no Matsubara|1=Miho no Matsubara}} has the ``Hagoromo no Matsu'', and from Hagoromo no Matsu to the head of Miho Shrine there is a line of pine trees, but this tree-lined road is It is said to be the path for the gods who descended from the pine trees to reach Miho Shrine, and is called the ``Kami no Michi'' {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. Even today, during the Tsutsugayu Shinto ritual, a ceremony is held on the shore to welcome the gods, and at that time, the Himorogi that the gods rely on are brought into the precincts through a row of pine trees.{{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. From these, the rituals at Miho Shrine are thought to be part of the ``Eternal Faith'' that welcomes the gods from the ``{{ill|lt=Eternal Country|Q11060259|WD=Q11060259|lt=Eternal Country|1=Tokoyo no kuni}}'' beyond the sea.{{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. === Outline === In the {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|lt=Kokukushi|1=Rikkokushi}}, the name of the god is ``Mikugami'', and {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}}7 years ({{ill|lt=865|Q30350|WD=Q30350|1=865}}) from the lower junior fifth rank to the upper junior fifth rank, later {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|WD=Q747451|1=Gangyō}}3 years ({{ja There is an article that states that {{ill|lt=Divine Grade|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} was promoted to the lower rank of Shogoi in 879|Q25001}}.{{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. {{ill|lt=extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}} It is described as ``Miho Shrine'' in Rubara County ({{ill|lt=Ibara District|Q988501|WD=Q988501|1=Ihara District, Shizuoka}}) and is listed as {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}.In ``{{ill|lt=Suruga Province Fudoki|Q11668491|WD=Q11668491|1=Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}'', it is written that ``Japanese Bushihohoku Kansei, Shikken Keida Gohyakuune, Tamekuni no Sangu Shrine.'' Therefore, our company is {{jalink|Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha Shrine|Q653180} in Suruga Province. } ({{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}}), it is presumed to have held the position of the third shrine after {{ill|lt=Toyozumi Shrine|Q18337536|WD=Q18337536|1=Toyozumi Shrine}} (Ninomiya) {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=151-152}}. However, the basis for writing this in the same article is not clear {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=151-152}}.Among the historical materials of the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}}, there is a crocodile mouth in the collection of Iimurono Shrine, dated 3 years ({{ill|lt=1319|Q5767|WD=Q5767|1=1319}}) {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. At this crocodile entrance, it looks like ``Miho Daimyojin,'' and it is said that it was dedicated by the priest's son and wife {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. In addition, in {{ill|lt=Oan|Q1146245|WD=Q1146245|1=Ōan}}3 ({{ill|lt=1370|Q6217|WD=Q6217|1=1370}}), Date Keimune was ordered to prohibit the felling of sacred trees and search for violators, and {{ill|lt=Koji (Japan)|Q1143910|WD=Q1143910|lt=Koji|1=Kōji (Muromachi period)}} In the 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1557|Q6595|WD=Q6595|1=1557}}), {{ill|lt=Yamashina Yotsugu|Q11470822|WD=Q11470822|1=Yamashina Tokitsugu}} was {{ill|lt=now At the recommendation of Kawa Yoshimoto|Q1054305|WD=Q1054305|1=Imagawa Yoshimoto}}, he visited a shrine marked as ``Miura no Daimyojin'' and paid his respects to the Ota clan (Ota clan) of Negi.{{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. In {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 5th year ({{ill|lt=1577|Q6704|WD=Q6704|1=1577}}), Kisaburo Ota was appointed as the chief priest of Miho, and the Daimyojin offering of the shrine, Matsubaraura et al., was relieved.{{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}.In the {{ill|lt=Early modern period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}, 106 stones from the three villages of Miho, Orito, and Betsufu were given as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shuinchi|1=UNKNOWN}} by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, and the shrine building was was also maintained, but it is said to have been destroyed by fire in {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}}8 ({{ill|lt=1668|Q7595|WD=Q7595|1=1668}}) {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} It was ranked as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, and in 1898|Q7848}}, it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}.{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Shiryo|1912}}. === God level === * Record of {{ill|lt=Shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} dedication in the {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|1=Rikkokushi}} era {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}} ** December 21, 7th year of {{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} ({{ill|lt=865|Q30350|WD=Q30350|1=865}}), from the lower junior fifth rank to the upper fifth rank (``{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'') - The spelling is ``Mirojin.'' ** April 5th, {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|WD=Q747451|1=Gangyō}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=879|Q25001|WD=Q25001|1=879}}), from the top of the Jugoi to the lower of the Shogoi ("Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku") - Written as "Mikujin". * After Six Kingdoms History ** Junior Second Rank (``Suruga Province Jinmyocho'') {{Sfn|Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries|2000|p=151-152}} - Written as "Miho Myojin." === Precincts === [[File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) honden.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main shrine (Shizuoka City designated cultural property)}}]] The shrine was built by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, but it was destroyed by fire in {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}}8 ({{ill|lt=1668|Q7595|WD=Q7595|1=1668}}) It is said to have been {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}, and was later built as a temporary shrine (karimiya) in the middle of the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} and continues to this day. The current main shrine is {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} and is designated as a cultural property by Shizuoka City. From the head of the shrine, a row of pine trees that are about 300 to 400 years old stretches for about 520 meters, called the "path of the gods," until the end of the row of pine trees {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Miho no Matsubara|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Noh song|Q8062662|WD=Q8062662|1=Yōkyoku}} ``Hagoromo no Matsu'', known for ``Hagoromo'', stands {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. The pine trees in Hagoromo have been replaced repeatedly due to withering and death, but it is presumed that the original pine trees were an extension of the tree-lined path {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. {{Small|It is said that a god descends from the land of eternal life.{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yorishiro|1=UNKNOWN}}. }} === Setsusha === [[File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) Koyasu-jinja.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Koyasu Shrine}}]] * Koyasu Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Susanoo no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inada Hime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} * 5 companies in Maiden East ** Gofukuno Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Chojiraha God|Q11653519|WD=Q11653519|lt=Choshiraha Mikoto|1=Nagashirahano no Kami}} ** Inari Shrine - Deities enshrined: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Uka no Mitama-kami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Omiyahime-kami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Ota Mikoto|Q97496337|WD=Q97496337|1=Ōta-no-Mikoto}} ** Kofudayu Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Koshiro Nushi no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} ** Isomae Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukuhikomakami|1=UNKNOWN}} ** Utsurei Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Koou Utreimiko|UNKNOWN|lt=Kou Utreimyo}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kou Utreimyo|1=UNKNOWN}} * 3 companies west of the main hall ** Hachiman Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} ** Yakumo Shrine - Enshrined deity: Susano no Mikoto ** Shinmeisha - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}} * Haguruma Shrine - Enshrined in the pine tree of Hagoromo. File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) massha-1.JPG|5 subsidiary companies File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) massha-2.JPG|3 subsidiary companies File:Haguruma shrine.jpg|Haguruma shrine === Festival === ; {{ill|lt=regular festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} : {{ill|lt=November 1st|Q2997|WD=Q2997|1=November 1}} The most important festival of the year. During the festival, in addition to the ritual of hot water, a shrine maiden dance called ``Hagoromo no Mai'' is dedicated at the Maiden. This hagoromo dance is also dedicated at the autumn festival. ; Tsutsugayu festival : "Tsukayusai". A festival to pray for a good harvest that is held from the night of {{ill|lt=February 14th|Q2332|WD=Q2332|1=February 14}} to {{ill|lt=February 15th|Q2334|WD=Q2334|lt=15th|1=February 15}}. As part of the ritual, first a large cauldron is set up in front of the shrine and preparations are made to boil porridge. Then, in the middle of the night, a shrine is set up on the beach to welcome the gods, and a ceremony is performed by singing prayers without using any bonfires. The welcomed god resides in a Himorogi, and the Shinto priest carries the Himorogi through a row of pine trees to reach the shrine grounds. Afterwards, porridge is boiled in a large pot in front of the shrine, a bamboo tube is placed in it, and the amount of porridge in the tube is used to predict the year's crop. {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}} === Cultural assets === === Important cultural property (designated by the country) === * Tachi Mumei (attached to Itomaki Tachi Koshirae) (craft) *: Written by {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}. The blade is 95.2 cm long and the curve is 4.2 cm . Designated on April 30, 1921 (Taisho 10) {{Nationally Designated Cultural Properties Database|201|6198|Tachi Mumei}}. === Cultural property designated by Shizuoka City === *Tangible cultural property ** Main shrine (2 ridge tags, 1 pair of guardian dogs) (building) - Designated on February 23, 1996 {{PDFlink|[http://www.city.shizuoka.jp/000719631.pdf List of city-designated cultural properties]}} (Shizuoka City homepage). . ** 2 bamboo blinds (craft) - Designated on November 21, 1988 . === Others === * Rip of Feathers *: A slit that is said to be a piece of hagoromo that appears in the legend of hagoromo . It is believed to be a piece of Hire/Momo from over 1,200 years ago, and it is also assumed that it originated from the continent based on the pattern of the textile. * Hagoromo flute - A flute played during the Hagoromo dance. * Miho old picture map * Sanhoshaki === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Shizuoka {{ill|lt=Surugakuni|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Sannomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}
Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} |Alternative name = Miho Daimyojin |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=November 1st|Q2997|WD=Q2997|1=November 1}} |Shinto ritual = Tsutsugayu Festival ({{ill|lt=February 14th|Q2332|WD=Q2332|1=February 14}}-{{ill|lt=February 14th|Q2332|WD=Q2332|lt=15th|1=February 14}})|Map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|lat_deg=35|lat_min=0|lat_sec=0.40|lon_deg=138|lon_min=31|lon_sec=15.16|label=Miho Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|0|0.40|N|138|31|15.16|E}}}} }} [[File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] ``Miho Shrine'' is located in {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Shizuoka City|Q174691|WD=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}}{{ill|lt=Shimizu Ward|Q79385|WD=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}} Miho. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Sannomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}, {{ill|lt=modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}}.It is known as a shrine related to ``Hagoromo no Matsu'' in {{ill|lt=Miho no Matsubara|Q869275|WD=Q869275|lt=Miho no Matsubara|1=Miho no Matsubara}}. The character ``Miho'' is also used for ``Miho'', ``Miho'', and ``Miho'' {{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Magazine Materials|1912}}. The shrine precincts and the approach (the path of the gods) are {{ill|lt=Cultural Heritage Sites (World Heritage)|Q210272|WD=Q210272|lt=World Cultural Heritage|1=Cultural heritage}} [http://www.shizuoka-citypromotion.jp/mihonomatsubara/learn/area.html Constituent assets Miho no Matsubara Range] (Shizuoka City). . === Enshrined God === The following two deities are enshrined. . :* '''{{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ookunushi|1=Ōkuninushi}}''' (Oanamuchi Mikoto, {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}}) - Also known as ``Mihotsuhiko no Mikoto.'' :* '''{{ill|lt=Mihotsuhime|Q6378698|WD=Q6378698|lt=Mihotsuhime no Mikoto|1=Mihotsuhime-no-Mikoto}}''' (Mihotsuhime no Mikoto) In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmeicho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmeicho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, there is one deity enshrined. Also, in "Suruga Zoshi", the two pillars are "Miho Tsuhiko" and "Miho Tsuhime" {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. === History === === Founding === [[File:Port of Shimizu and Miho Coast Areas Aerial photograph.jpg|thumb|220px|{{center|Aerial photograph around the Miho Peninsula}}{{Small|Composite of 12 photos taken in 1983. {{National Aerial Photography}}. The corner to the right of the center of the image is the precincts of Miho Shrine, and a row of pine trees extends from the precincts to {{ill|lt=Miho no Matsubara|Q869275|WD=Q869275|1=Miho no Matsubara}} on the coast. }}]] Founding date is unknown. In "Suruga Zoshi", it is said that {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} donated government money and shrine territory by imperial order to Mihosaki, Izumo Province (currently {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Matsue City|Q207321|WD=Q207321|1=Matsue}}It is also said that the deity was relocated from Mihonoseki Town), but it is not clear {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}.{{ill|lt=Miho no Matsubara|Q869275|WD=Q869275|lt=Miho no Matsubara|1=Miho no Matsubara}} has the ``Hagoromo no Matsu'', and from Hagoromo no Matsu to the head of Miho Shrine there is a line of pine trees, but this tree-lined road is It is said to be the path for the gods who descended from the pine trees to reach Miho Shrine, and is called the ``Kami no Michi'' {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. Even today, during the Tsutsugayu Shinto ritual, a ceremony is held on the shore to welcome the gods, and at that time, the Himorogi that the gods rely on are brought into the precincts through a row of pine trees.{{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. From these, the rituals at Miho Shrine are thought to be part of the ``Eternal Faith'' that welcomes the gods from the ``{{ill|lt=Eternal Country|Q11060259|WD=Q11060259|lt=Eternal Country|1=Tokoyo no kuni}}'' beyond the sea.{{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. === Outline === In the {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|lt=Kokukushi|1=Rikkokushi}}, the name of the god is ``Mikugami'', and {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}}7 years ({{ill|lt=865|Q30350|WD=Q30350|1=865}}) from the lower junior fifth rank to the upper junior fifth rank, later {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|WD=Q747451|1=Gangyō}}3 years ({{ja There is an article that states that {{ill|lt=Divine Grade|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} was promoted to the lower rank of Shogoi in 879|Q25001}}.{{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. {{ill|lt=extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|WD=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}} It is described as ``Miho Shrine'' in Rubara County ({{ill|lt=Ibara District|Q988501|WD=Q988501|1=Ihara District, Shizuoka}}) and is listed as {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}.In ``{{ill|lt=Suruga Province Fudoki|Q11668491|WD=Q11668491|1=Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}'', it is written that ``Japanese Bushihohoku Kansei, Shikken Keida Gohyakuune, Tamekuni no Sangu Shrine.'' Therefore, our company is {{jalink|Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha Shrine|Q653180} in Suruga Province. } ({{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}}), it is presumed to have held the position of the third shrine after {{ill|lt=Toyozumi Shrine|Q18337536|WD=Q18337536|1=Toyozumi Shrine}} (Ninomiya) {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=151-152}}. However, the basis for writing this in the same article is not clear {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=151-152}}.Among the historical materials of the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}}, there is a crocodile mouth in the collection of Iimurono Shrine, dated 3 years ({{ill|lt=1319|Q5767|WD=Q5767|1=1319}}) {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. At this crocodile entrance, it looks like ``Miho Daimyojin,'' and it is said that it was dedicated by the priest's son and wife {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. In addition, in {{ill|lt=Oan|Q1146245|WD=Q1146245|1=Ōan}}3 ({{ill|lt=1370|Q6217|WD=Q6217|1=1370}}), Date Keimune was ordered to prohibit the felling of sacred trees and search for violators, and {{ill|lt=Koji (Japan)|Q1143910|WD=Q1143910|lt=Koji|1=Kōji (Muromachi period)}} In the 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1557|Q6595|WD=Q6595|1=1557}}), {{ill|lt=Yamashina Yotsugu|Q11470822|WD=Q11470822|1=Yamashina Tokitsugu}} was {{ill|lt=now At the recommendation of Kawa Yoshimoto|Q1054305|WD=Q1054305|1=Imagawa Yoshimoto}}, he visited a shrine marked as ``Miura no Daimyojin'' and paid his respects to the Ota clan (Ota clan) of Negi.{{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. In {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 5th year ({{ill|lt=1577|Q6704|WD=Q6704|1=1577}}), Kisaburo Ota was appointed as the chief priest of Miho, and the Daimyojin offering of the shrine, Matsubaraura et al., was relieved.{{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}.In the {{ill|lt=Early modern period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}, 106 stones from the three villages of Miho, Orito, and Betsufu were given as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shuinchi|1=UNKNOWN}} by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, and the shrine building was was also maintained, but it is said to have been destroyed by fire in {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}}8 ({{ill|lt=1668|Q7595|WD=Q7595|1=1668}}) {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}. After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} It was ranked as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, and in 1898|Q7848}}, it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}.{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Shiryo|1912}}. === God level === * Record of {{ill|lt=Shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} dedication in the {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|1=Rikkokushi}} era {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}} ** December 21, 7th year of {{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} ({{ill|lt=865|Q30350|WD=Q30350|1=865}}), from the lower junior fifth rank to the upper fifth rank (``{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'') - The spelling is ``Mirojin.'' ** April 5th, {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|WD=Q747451|1=Gangyō}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=879|Q25001|WD=Q25001|1=879}}), from the top of the Jugoi to the lower of the Shogoi ("Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku") - Written as "Mikujin". * After Six Kingdoms History ** Junior Second Rank (``Suruga Province Jinmyocho'') {{Sfn|Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries|2000|p=151-152}} - Written as "Miho Myojin." === Precincts === [[File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) honden.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main shrine (Shizuoka City designated cultural property)}}]] The shrine was built by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, but it was destroyed by fire in {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}}8 ({{ill|lt=1668|Q7595|WD=Q7595|1=1668}}) It is said to have been {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2000}}, and was later built as a temporary shrine (karimiya) in the middle of the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} and continues to this day. The current main shrine is {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} and is designated as a cultural property by Shizuoka City. From the head of the shrine, a row of pine trees that are about 300 to 400 years old stretches for about 520 meters, called the "path of the gods," until the end of the row of pine trees {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Miho no Matsubara|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Noh song|Q8062662|WD=Q8062662|1=Yōkyoku}} ``Hagoromo no Matsu'', known for ``Hagoromo'', stands {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. The pine trees in Hagoromo have been replaced repeatedly due to withering and death, but it is presumed that the original pine trees were an extension of the tree-lined path {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}}. File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) shaden.JPG|Hotel File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) sandou.JPG|Pine trees along the approach (God's path)File:Miho-no-Matsubara Hagoromo-no-matsu.JPG|Hagoromo no Matsu
{{Small|It is said that a god descends from the land of eternal life.{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yorishiro|1=UNKNOWN}}. }} === Setsusha === [[File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) Koyasu-jinja.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Koyasu Shrine}}]] * Koyasu Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Susanoo no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inada Hime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} * 5 companies in Maiden East ** Gofukuno Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Chojiraha God|Q11653519|WD=Q11653519|lt=Choshiraha Mikoto|1=Nagashirahano no Kami}} ** Inari Shrine - Deities enshrined: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Uka no Mitama-kami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Omiyahime-kami|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Ota Mikoto|Q97496337|WD=Q97496337|1=Ōta-no-Mikoto}} ** Kofudayu Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Koshiro Nushi no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} ** Isomae Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukuhikomakami|1=UNKNOWN}} ** Utsurei Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Koou Utreimiko|UNKNOWN|lt=Kou Utreimyo}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kou Utreimyo|1=UNKNOWN}} * 3 companies west of the main hall ** Hachiman Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} ** Yakumo Shrine - Enshrined deity: Susano no Mikoto ** Shinmeisha - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}} * Haguruma Shrine - Enshrined in the pine tree of Hagoromo. File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) massha-1.JPG|5 subsidiary companies File:Miho-jinja (Shizuoka) massha-2.JPG|3 subsidiary companies File:Haguruma shrine.jpg|Haguruma shrine === Festival === ; {{ill|lt=regular festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} : {{ill|lt=November 1st|Q2997|WD=Q2997|1=November 1}} The most important festival of the year. During the festival, in addition to the ritual of hot water, a shrine maiden dance called ``Hagoromo no Mai'' is dedicated at the Maiden. This hagoromo dance is also dedicated at the autumn festival. ; Tsutsugayu festival : "Tsukayusai". A festival to pray for a good harvest that is held from the night of {{ill|lt=February 14th|Q2332|WD=Q2332|1=February 14}} to {{ill|lt=February 15th|Q2334|WD=Q2334|lt=15th|1=February 15}}. As part of the ritual, first a large cauldron is set up in front of the shrine and preparations are made to boil porridge. Then, in the middle of the night, a shrine is set up on the beach to welcome the gods, and a ceremony is performed by singing prayers without using any bonfires. The welcomed god resides in a Himorogi, and the Shinto priest carries the Himorogi through a row of pine trees to reach the shrine grounds. Afterwards, porridge is boiled in a large pot in front of the shrine, a bamboo tube is placed in it, and the amount of porridge in the tube is used to predict the year's crop. {{Sfn|Miho Shrine (Gods)|1987}} === Cultural assets === === Important cultural property (designated by the country) === * Tachi Mumei (attached to Itomaki Tachi Koshirae) (craft) *: Written by {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}. The blade is 95.2 cm long and the curve is 4.2 cm . Designated on April 30, 1921 (Taisho 10) {{Nationally Designated Cultural Properties Database|201|6198|Tachi Mumei}}. === Cultural property designated by Shizuoka City === *Tangible cultural property ** Main shrine (2 ridge tags, 1 pair of guardian dogs) (building) - Designated on February 23, 1996 {{PDFlink|[http://www.city.shizuoka.jp/000719631.pdf List of city-designated cultural properties]}} (Shizuoka City homepage). . ** 2 bamboo blinds (craft) - Designated on November 21, 1988 . === Others === * Rip of Feathers *: A slit that is said to be a piece of hagoromo that appears in the legend of hagoromo . It is believed to be a piece of Hire/Momo from over 1,200 years ago, and it is also assumed that it originated from the continent based on the pattern of the textile. * Hagoromo flute - A flute played during the Hagoromo dance. * Miho old picture map * Sanhoshaki === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Shizuoka City|Q174691|WD=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}}{{ill|lt=Shimizu Ward|Q79385|WD=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}}Miho 1073 '''Access''' * {{ill|lt=Tokai Passenger Railway|Q513679|WD=Q513679|1=Central Japan Railway Company}} (JR Tokai) {{ill|lt=Tokaido Main Line|Q1190152|WD=Q1190152|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}} {{ill|lt=Shimizu Station (Shizuoka Prefecture)|Q863065|WD=Q863065|lt=Shimizu Station|1=Shimizu Station (Shizuoka)}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Railway|Q5362040|WD=Q5362040|1=Shizuoka Railway}}{{ill|lt=Shizuoka Railway Shizuoka Shimizu Line|Q864249|WD=Q864249|lt=Shizuoka Shimizu Line|1=Shizuoka Railway Shizuoka–Shimizu Line}} {{ill|lt=Shin-Shimizu Station|Q7497132|WD=Q7497132|1=Shin-Shimizu Station}} ** Bus: Take {{ill|lt=Shizutetsu Just Line|Q11268847|WD=Q11268847|1=Shizutetsu Justline}} (bound for Miho) and get off at "Miho Matsubara Iriguchi" bus stop (about 25 minutes on board, about 5 minutes' walk after getting off) === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine history book * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|author=|year=1912|chapter=Miho Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Prefecture Gosha Meiji Shrine Materials|Q11486103|WD=Q11486103|1=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Materials|1912}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088244/884}} "Meiji Shrine Journal Materials Fuken Gosha Part 1"] (National Diet Library Digital Collection) Reference frames 884-885. * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor={{ill|lt=Kenichi
